1:
"And you hath he quickened, who were dead in trespasses and sins;" The witnesses note the spiritually dead made alive. On "you hath he quickened," the grammatical voices note the new life. Barnes and Robertson note the great theme (the words "hath he quickened" supplied from v.5): God has made the Ephesians spiritually alive — "quickened (made alive)" those who were lifeless in sin; spiritual life is God's gift.

On "who were dead in trespasses and sins," the witnesses note the former state. Barnes and Calvin note the condition from which they were raised: "dead in trespasses (paraptoma — fallings, transgressions) and sins (hamartia)" — not merely sick or weak, but dead, spiritually lifeless, helpless and unresponsive toward God; the strongest statement of human depravity.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the depth: spiritual death means total inability to quicken oneself; as a corpse cannot raise itself, so the dead in sin must be made alive by God. The verse displays the spiritually dead made alive.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God has "quickened (made alive)" the Ephesians, spiritual life his gift (Barnes, Robertson); they were "dead in trespasses (transgressions) and sins" — not sick but dead, spiritually lifeless and unresponsive toward God, the strongest statement of depravity (Barnes, Calvin); spiritual death meaning total inability to quicken oneself, as a corpse cannot raise itself (Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays the dead made alive. The verse marks the quickening: made alive. The verse displays the state: dead in sins. The verse displays the depravity: spiritual death. The verse displays the inability: total. The quickening displays God raising the spiritually dead. The verse displays the dead: in sins. The verse displays the life: God's gift. The verse displays the helplessness: of the dead. The spiritually dead made alive display the depth of grace: God has quickened those who were dead in trespasses and sins — not merely weak but lifeless, helpless to raise themselves — giving them spiritual life by his own power. The verse displays the new life from death, against the former walk in sin (vv.2-3).
2:
"Wherein in time past ye walked according to the course of this world, according to the prince of the power of the air, the spirit that now worketh in the children of disobedience:" The witnesses note the former walk under the world and the prince of the air. On "ye walked according to the course of this world," the grammatical voices note the worldly walk. Barnes and Robertson note that in their dead state the Ephesians "walked (lived, conducted themselves) according to the course (aion — the age, the spirit and fashion) of this world" — following the world's sinful customs and values.

On "according to the prince of the power of the air," the witnesses note the satanic rule. Barnes and Calvin note that they walked according to "the prince of the power of the air" — Satan, the ruler of the evil powers (here pictured as in the air, the lower heavens), the prince of demons who holds sway over the disobedient.

On "the spirit that now worketh in the children of disobedience," the grammatical-historical note Satan's operation: this evil spirit "now worketh (is energetically at work) in the children of disobedience" — Satan actively operates in those who refuse to obey God; the unconverted are under his influence. The verse displays the former walk under the world and the prince of the air.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: in their dead state the Ephesians "walked according to the course (the age, fashion) of this world," following its sinful customs (Barnes, Robertson); "according to the prince of the power of the air" — Satan, ruler of the evil powers, prince of demons holding sway over the disobedient (Barnes, Calvin); "the spirit that now worketh in the children of disobedience," Satan actively operating in those who refuse to obey God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the former walk. The verse marks the world: its sinful course. The verse displays the prince: of the air. The verse displays the spirit: at work. The verse displays the disobedient: under his sway. The walk displays the unconverted under the world and Satan. The verse displays the course: of this world. The verse displays the prince: Satan. The verse displays the working: in the disobedient. The former walk under the world and the prince of the air displays the bondage from which grace delivers: in their spiritual death the Ephesians lived by the sinful fashion of the world and under Satan, the prince of the power of the air, who works in all who disobey God. The verse displays the old life under sin and Satan, shared by all in their depravity (v.3).
3:
"Among whom also we all had our conversation in times past in the lusts of our flesh, fulfilling the desires of the flesh and of the mind; and were by nature the children of wrath, even as others." The witnesses note all by nature children of wrath, living in the lusts of the flesh. On "we all had our conversation... in the lusts of our flesh," the grammatical voices note the universal sin. Barnes and Robertson note Paul includes himself and all ("we all"): they had their "conversation (manner of life) in times past in the lusts of the flesh, fulfilling the desires (wills) of the flesh and of the mind" — gratifying both bodily appetites and the corrupt inclinations of the mind.

On "were by nature the children of wrath," the witnesses note the natural state. Barnes and Calvin note the weighty statement: "by nature (physei — by birth, by our natural condition) the children of wrath" — by nature, not merely by act, all are objects of God's wrath, deserving condemnation; a key text for original sin and native depravity.

On "even as others," the Reformed Calvin notes the universality: this is true of "even as others (the rest)" — Jew and Gentile alike, none exempt; all share the same fallen nature and the same liability to wrath. The verse displays all by nature children of wrath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul includes all — "we all had our conversation in the lusts of the flesh, fulfilling the desires of the flesh and of the mind," gratifying bodily appetites and corrupt mind (Barnes, Robertson); "by nature (by birth) the children of wrath," all by nature objects of God's wrath, a key text for original sin (Barnes, Calvin); "even as others," Jew and Gentile alike, none exempt (Calvin). The verse displays children of wrath. The verse marks the sin: lusts of the flesh and mind. The verse displays the nature: children of wrath. The verse displays the universality: even as others. The verse displays the doctrine: native depravity. The state displays all by nature deserving wrath. The verse displays the lusts: indulged. The verse displays the wrath: deserved. The verse displays the nature: fallen. All by nature children of wrath, living in the lusts of the flesh, display the universal fallen condition: Paul and all, in their former life, fulfilled the desires of flesh and mind, and were by nature — by birth — children of wrath, deserving condemnation, even as the rest of mankind. The verse displays the depth of native depravity, met by the great "But God" of mercy (vv.4-5).
4:
"But God, who is rich in mercy, for his great love wherewith he loved us," The witnesses note the great turn: God rich in mercy and great love. On "but God, who is rich in mercy," the grammatical voices note the decisive contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the glorious turn ("but God") that breaks into the dark picture of human ruin: "God, who is rich (wealthy, abounding) in mercy" — over against man's deadness and wrath stands God's abundant mercy; the hinge of the passage.

On "for his great love wherewith he loved us," the witnesses note the motive. Barnes and Calvin note the spring of salvation: "his great love wherewith he loved us" — it was God's own great love, freely set on the unlovely and dead, that moved him to act; love and mercy, not any merit in us, are the cause.

The eastern Cyril and puritan Henry note the wonder: that God should love and have mercy on those who were dead in sin and children of wrath is the marvel of grace; the "but God" turns the whole from ruin to salvation. The verse displays the great turn: God rich in mercy and great love.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the glorious turn ("but God") breaking into the picture of ruin — "God, who is rich (abounding) in mercy," over against man's deadness stands God's abundant mercy (Barnes, Robertson); the spring of salvation being "his great love wherewith he loved us," love freely set on the dead and unlovely, not any merit (Barnes, Calvin); the marvel that God loves the children of wrath (Cyril, Henry). The verse displays God rich in mercy. The verse marks the turn: but God. The verse displays the mercy: rich. The verse displays the motive: his great love. The verse displays the wonder: love for the dead. The turn displays God's mercy breaking into man's ruin. The verse displays the God: rich in mercy. The verse displays the love: great. The verse displays the contrast: ruin to grace. The great turn — God rich in mercy and great love — displays the hinge of the whole passage: against the dark backdrop of human deadness and wrath, the "but God" breaks in, for God, abounding in mercy and moved by his own great love, set his favour on the dead and unlovely. The verse displays the mercy and love of God, which quickened us with Christ (v.5).
5:
"Even when we were dead in sins, hath quickened us together with Christ, (by grace ye are saved;)" The witnesses note the dead quickened together with Christ by grace. On "even when we were dead in sins, hath quickened us together with Christ," the grammatical voices note the new life in union. Barnes and Robertson note the heart of the matter: "even when we were dead in sins (at the very point of our deadness, not after we improved) God hath quickened us together with Christ (synezoopoiesen)" — made us alive together with the risen Christ, our new life flowing from union with him.

On "together with Christ," the witnesses note the union. Barnes and Calvin note that believers are quickened "together with Christ" — their spiritual resurrection is bound up with his; as he was raised, so they are raised to new life in him.

On "(by grace ye are saved)," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the parenthesis: Paul breaks in with the great summary — "by grace ye are saved" — unable to mention this quickening without ascribing it wholly to grace; salvation is entirely of free favour. The verse displays the dead quickened together with Christ by grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "even when we were dead in sins (at the very point of deadness) God hath quickened us together with Christ," making believers alive with the risen Christ, new life from union with him (Barnes, Robertson); their spiritual resurrection bound up with his (Barnes, Calvin); and the parenthesis "by grace ye are saved" ascribing the whole to free favour (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays the dead quickened by grace. The verse marks the deadness: even when dead. The verse displays the quickening: together with Christ. The verse displays the union: with the risen Christ. The verse displays the grace: by which saved. The quickening displays the dead raised with Christ by grace. The verse displays the dead: made alive. The verse displays the Christ: the source. The verse displays the grace: free. The dead quickened together with Christ by grace display the marvel of salvation: at the very point of their deadness, God made believers alive together with the risen Christ — their new life flowing from union with him — and it is all, Paul breaks in to say, by grace. The verse displays the resurrection-life in Christ, raised and seated with him (v.6).
6:
"And hath raised us up together, and made us sit together in heavenly places in Christ Jesus:" The brief verse notes believers raised and seated with Christ in the heavenly places. The grammatical voices note the exaltation. Barnes and Robertson note that God has "raised us up together (synegeiren — co-raised)" with Christ and "made us sit together (synekathisen — co-seated) in heavenly places in Christ Jesus" — believers share in Christ's resurrection and his exaltation, by virtue of union with him.

On "in heavenly places in Christ Jesus," the witnesses note the present and certain reality. Barnes and Calvin note that this is, in a sense, already true: in Christ, their head, believers are already raised and seated in the heavenly realm; what is true of the head is reckoned true of the members, the future glory guaranteed in him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the hope: the believer's exaltation with Christ is begun in spiritual life and assured of consummation; the seating with him is the pledge of final glory. The verse displays believers raised and seated with Christ in the heavenly places.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God has "raised us up together (co-raised)" with Christ and "made us sit together (co-seated) in heavenly places in Christ Jesus," believers sharing his resurrection and exaltation by union with him (Barnes, Robertson); already true in Christ their head, the members reckoned as the head, future glory guaranteed (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes the seating is the pledge of final glory. The verse displays raised and seated with Christ. The verse marks the raising: together with Christ. The verse displays the seating: in heavenly places. The verse displays the union: in Christ. The verse displays the pledge: of glory. The exaltation displays believers sharing Christ's risen and seated life. The verse displays the raising: co-raised. The verse displays the seating: co-seated. The verse displays the place: heavenly. Believers raised and seated with Christ in the heavenly places display the height of their union with him: as God raised and seated the risen Christ, so believers, joined to him, are already raised up and seated with him in the heavenly realm — their present spiritual life and the pledge of their final glory. The verse displays the exaltation with Christ, to display God's grace forever (v.7).
7:
"That in the ages to come he might shew the exceeding riches of his grace in his kindness toward us through Christ Jesus." The brief verse notes the eternal display of God's grace toward believers. The grammatical voices note the design. Barnes and Robertson note God's purpose in saving and exalting believers: "that in the ages to come (the endless future) he might shew (display, exhibit) the exceeding (surpassing) riches of his grace" — believers are an eternal exhibition of God's grace.

On "in his kindness toward us through Christ Jesus," the witnesses note the manner. Barnes and Calvin note that this grace is shown "in his kindness (goodness) toward us through Christ Jesus" — God's saving kindness, displayed in the redeemed, channelled through Christ; the saved are the trophies of his grace forever.

The grammatical-historical note the scope: throughout eternity the redeemed will be the proof and display of the surpassing riches of God's grace; their salvation glorifies him without end. The verse displays the eternal display of God's grace toward believers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God's purpose "that in the ages to come (the endless future) he might shew (display) the exceeding (surpassing) riches of his grace," believers an eternal exhibition of grace (Barnes, Robertson); shown "in his kindness toward us through Christ Jesus," God's saving kindness channelled through Christ, the saved his trophies forever (Barnes, Calvin). The grammatical-historical note the redeemed eternally display his grace. The verse displays the eternal display of grace. The verse marks the design: shew his grace. The verse displays the scope: ages to come. The verse displays the riches: exceeding. The verse displays the channel: through Christ. The design displays the redeemed as eternal trophies of grace. The verse displays the grace: surpassing. The verse displays the kindness: toward us. The verse displays the ages: to come. The eternal display of God's grace toward believers displays the ultimate purpose of their salvation: throughout the endless ages to come, God will exhibit in the redeemed the surpassing riches of his grace, shown in his kindness toward them through Christ Jesus — they are the everlasting trophies of his grace. The verse displays the eternal glory of grace, summed in salvation by grace through faith (vv.8-9).
8:
"For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God:" The witnesses note salvation by grace through faith, the gift of God — and the grammatical crux of the gift. On "by grace are ye saved through faith," the grammatical voices note the two parts. Barnes and Robertson note the great statement: "by grace ye are saved (have been and continue saved) through faith" — grace is the source and moving cause (God's part), faith the instrument by which it is received (our part); salvation flows from grace and is laid hold of by faith.

On "and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God," interpreters divide over the antecedent of "that." The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, with the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Clarke, note that "that" (touto, neuter) does not grammatically agree with "faith" (pistis, feminine), and so refers not to faith specifically but to the whole act/fact of salvation by grace through faith — this salvation is not of ourselves but God's gift. A minority (Chrysostom, Theodoret, Beza, Doddridge) refer "that" to faith — "faith is the gift of God." Barnes adds that, whatever the grammar, faith is in another sense God's gift (the inclination produced by the Spirit), though the act of believing is the believer's own; Clarke distinguishes the God-given power to believe from the man's own act of believing.

The witnesses note the aim: all is excluded that is "of yourselves," that salvation may be wholly God's gift. The verse displays salvation by grace through faith, the gift of God.

The witnesses agree the core: "by grace ye are saved through faith" — grace the source (God's part), faith the instrument (our part) — and salvation "not of yourselves" but "the gift of God" (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). On the antecedent of "that," most (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke) hold "that" (neuter) cannot refer to "faith" (feminine) but to the whole salvation-by-grace, which is God's gift; a minority (Chrysostom, Theodoret, Beza, Doddridge) refer it to faith. Barnes notes faith is in another sense God's gift, though the act of believing is ours; Clarke distinguishes the God-given power to believe from the act. The verse displays the gift of salvation. The verse marks the grace: the source. The verse displays the faith: the instrument. The verse displays the debate: the gift is salvation or faith. The verse displays the exclusion: not of ourselves. The statement displays salvation wholly God's gift. The verse displays the grace: God's part. The verse displays the faith: ours. The verse displays the gift: of God. Salvation by grace through faith, the gift of God, displays the heart of the gospel and a famous grammatical crux: salvation flows from grace and is received by faith, and is "not of yourselves" but God's gift — "that" referring, most hold, to the whole salvation by grace (not to faith specifically), though faith too, in another sense, is God's gift. The verse displays the gift of grace, excluding all works and boasting (v.9).
9:
"Not of works, lest any man should boast." The brief verse notes salvation not of works, leaving no room for boasting. The grammatical voices note the exclusion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's emphatic negative: salvation is "not of works" — not procured, merited, or earned by any human works of any kind; the door to works-righteousness is wholly shut.

On "lest any man should boast," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note God's design in saving by grace and not works: "lest any man should boast (glory)" — that no one may have ground to boast in himself; if works could save, man would have something to glory in, but grace excludes all boasting.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comprehensiveness: Paul excludes not merely ceremonial works (as some claimed) but all works, the whole righteousness of man; the contrast of God and man, grace and works, leaves boasting no place. The verse displays salvation not of works, leaving no room for boasting.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: salvation is "not of works," not merited or earned by any human works, the door to works-righteousness wholly shut (Barnes, Robertson); God's design being "lest any man should boast," that none may glory in himself, grace excluding all boasting (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes Paul excludes all works, the whole righteousness of man, not merely ceremonies. The verse displays salvation not of works. The verse marks the exclusion: not of works. The verse displays the purpose: no boasting. The verse displays the comprehensiveness: all works. The verse displays the contrast: grace not merit. The exclusion displays grace leaving no room for boasting. The verse displays the works: excluded. The verse displays the boasting: silenced. The verse displays the grace: alone. Salvation not of works, leaving no room for boasting, displays the design of grace: salvation is not earned by any human works, that no one may have ground to boast in himself — grace, excluding all merit, shuts the mouth of human pride. The verse displays the exclusion of works and boasting, grounded in the believer as God's workmanship (v.10).
10:
"For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them." The witnesses note believers as God's workmanship, created unto good works. On "we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus," the grammatical voices note the new creation. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of the foregoing: "we are his workmanship (poiema — his making, his handiwork), created (a new creation) in Christ Jesus" — believers are wholly God's work, made new in Christ; salvation is God's creative act, not man's achievement.

On "created... unto good works," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note that they are created "unto good works" — good works are not the cause of salvation but its purpose and fruit; we are saved not by good works but for them.

On "which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the foreordaining: God "before ordained (prepared beforehand)" these good works, "that we should walk in them" — the believer's life of holiness is itself prepared and appointed by God; grace produces the very works it does not depend on. The verse displays believers as God's workmanship, created unto good works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "we are his workmanship (his handiwork), created (a new creation) in Christ Jesus," believers wholly God's work, made new in Christ, salvation his creative act (Barnes, Robertson); "created unto good works," good works the purpose and fruit of salvation, not its cause — saved not by them but for them (Barnes, Calvin); God "before ordained (prepared beforehand)" these works "that we should walk in them," the life of holiness appointed by God (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays God's workmanship. The verse marks the creation: his workmanship in Christ. The verse displays the purpose: unto good works. The verse displays the order: saved for, not by, works. The verse displays the foreordaining: prepared beforehand. The workmanship displays believers created by grace for good works. The verse displays the making: God's. The verse displays the works: the fruit. The verse displays the walk: ordained. Believers as God's workmanship, created unto good works, display the right place of works in salvation: believers are God's own handiwork, a new creation in Christ — saved not by good works but created for them, which God prepared beforehand that they should walk in them. The verse displays the new creation unto holiness, closing the doctrine of grace before the union of Jew and Gentile (vv.11-22).
11:
"Wherefore remember, that ye being in time past Gentiles in the flesh, who are called Uncircumcision by that which is called the Circumcision in the flesh made by hands;" The witnesses note the Gentiles' former state, called Uncircumcision. On "remember, that ye being in time past Gentiles in the flesh," the grammatical voices note the call to memory. Barnes and Robertson note Paul bids the Gentile believers "remember" their former condition: they were "Gentiles in the flesh" — by birth and outward state outside the covenant people.

On "called Uncircumcision by that which is called the Circumcision," the witnesses note the names. Barnes and Chrysostom note the contemptuous distinction: the Jews ("the Circumcision") called the Gentiles "Uncircumcision" — a term of reproach — though their own circumcision was merely "in the flesh, made by hands" (an outward, manual rite, not the inward circumcision of the heart).

The puritan Henry notes the purpose of remembering: Paul would have them recall their former alienation, that they may the more value the grace that brought them near. The verse displays the Gentiles' former state, called Uncircumcision.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul bids the Gentile believers "remember" their former condition — "Gentiles in the flesh," by birth outside the covenant (Barnes, Robertson); the Jews ("the Circumcision") called them "Uncircumcision" in reproach, though their own circumcision was merely "in the flesh, made by hands," an outward rite (Barnes, Chrysostom). Henry notes Paul would have them recall their alienation, to value grace. The verse displays the former state. The verse marks the memory: remember your past. The verse displays the state: Gentiles in the flesh. The verse displays the reproach: called Uncircumcision. The verse displays the rite: in the flesh, made by hands. The remembering displays the Gentiles' former alienation recalled. The verse displays the Gentiles: in the flesh. The verse displays the name: Uncircumcision. The verse displays the circumcision: outward. The Gentiles' former state, called Uncircumcision, displays the alienation Paul bids them remember: by birth they were Gentiles in the flesh, contemptuously called "Uncircumcision" by the Jews — whose own circumcision was but an outward, manual rite — that they might value the grace that changed their condition. The verse displays the Gentiles' former reproach, deepened into their lost estate (v.12).
12:
"That at that time ye were without Christ, being aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers from the covenants of promise, having no hope, and without God in the world:" The witnesses note the Gentiles' fivefold former lostness. On "without Christ... aliens from the commonwealth of Israel," the grammatical voices note the first privations. Barnes and Robertson note the dark catalogue of the Gentiles' former state: "without Christ (having no Messiah, no Saviour), aliens from the commonwealth (the citizenship, the polity) of Israel" — outside God's covenant nation.

On "strangers from the covenants of promise," the witnesses note the third. Barnes and Calvin note they were "strangers from the covenants of promise" — having no part in the covenants by which God promised redemption and blessing; the promises were not theirs.

On "having no hope, and without God in the world," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the climax: "having no hope (no well-founded expectation of future blessedness) and without God (atheoi — godless, having no true God) in the world" — the depth of Gentile misery, hopeless and Godless. The verse displays the Gentiles' fivefold former lostness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dark catalogue — "without Christ (no Saviour), aliens from the commonwealth of Israel" (outside God's covenant nation, Barnes, Robertson); "strangers from the covenants of promise," having no part in God's redemptive covenants (Barnes, Calvin); "having no hope, and without God (godless, no true God) in the world," hopeless and Godless, the depth of Gentile misery (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fivefold lostness. The verse marks the first: without Christ. The verse displays the alienation: from Israel. The verse displays the strangers: from the covenants. The verse displays the climax: no hope, without God. The catalogue displays the Gentiles' utter former lostness. The verse displays the Christless: state. The verse displays the hopeless: condition. The verse displays the Godless: world. The Gentiles' fivefold former lostness displays the depth from which grace raised them: without Christ, aliens from Israel's commonwealth, strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope, and without God in the world — the complete misery of the Gentile state before grace. The verse displays the lost estate of the Gentiles, reversed by the blood of Christ (v.13).
13:
"But now in Christ Jesus ye who sometimes were far off are made nigh by the blood of Christ." The witnesses note the far-off Gentiles brought near by Christ's blood. On "but now in Christ Jesus," the grammatical voices note the great reversal. Barnes and Robertson note the glorious turn ("but now") matching the "but God" of v.4: "in Christ Jesus ye who sometimes (formerly) were far off (distant from God, the covenant, and hope) are made nigh (brought near)" — the whole former distance reversed in Christ.

On "far off... made nigh," the witnesses note the change of position. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast: the Gentiles, once "far off" (the rabbinic term for those outside the covenant), are now "made nigh" — brought into nearness to God and into his people; the great distance abolished.

On "by the blood of Christ," the Reformed Calvin notes the means: this nearness is "by the blood of Christ" — his atoning death is the price and means by which the far-off are brought near; reconciliation is through the cross. The verse displays the far-off Gentiles brought near by Christ's blood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the glorious turn ("but now") — "in Christ Jesus ye who formerly were far off (distant from God and the covenant) are made nigh (brought near)," the former distance reversed (Barnes, Robertson); the Gentiles, once "far off," now "made nigh," brought into nearness to God and his people (Barnes, Calvin); "by the blood of Christ," his atoning death the means of nearness (Calvin). The verse displays the far brought near. The verse marks the turn: but now. The verse displays the former: far off. The verse displays the change: made nigh. The verse displays the means: the blood of Christ. The reversal displays the distant Gentiles brought near by the cross. The verse displays the far off: the Gentiles. The verse displays the nearness: granted. The verse displays the blood: the means. The far-off Gentiles brought near by Christ's blood display the great reversal of their lostness: those once far off from God, the covenant, and hope are now, in Christ Jesus, brought near — and this nearness is purchased by the blood of Christ, his atoning death. The verse displays the reconciliation by the cross, for Christ is our peace (v.14).
14:
"For he is our peace, who hath made both one, and hath broken down the middle wall of partition between us;" The witnesses note Christ our peace, breaking the middle wall between Jew and Gentile. On "he is our peace, who hath made both one," the grammatical voices note the reconciliation. Barnes and Robertson note that "he (Christ) is our peace" — not merely the maker but himself the peace — "who hath made both (Jew and Gentile) one" — uniting the two long-divided peoples into one body.

On "broken down the middle wall of partition," the witnesses note the barrier removed. Barnes and Calvin note the figure: Christ "hath broken down the middle wall of partition (the dividing wall, the fence)" — the barrier that separated Jew from Gentile; the image is drawn from the wall in the temple that shut Gentiles out of the inner courts, and signifies the ceremonial law that divided them.

The puritan Henry notes the peace: by removing the dividing barrier, Christ made peace between the two and reconciled both to God; he is the bond and the substance of their unity. The verse displays Christ our peace, breaking the middle wall.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "he (Christ) is our peace" — himself the peace, not merely its maker — "who hath made both (Jew and Gentile) one," uniting the divided peoples (Barnes, Robertson); "broken down the middle wall of partition (the dividing wall, the fence)," the barrier separating Jew from Gentile, imaged by the temple wall and signifying the ceremonial law (Barnes, Calvin); making peace and reconciling both (Henry). The verse displays Christ our peace. The verse marks the peace: Christ himself. The verse displays the union: both made one. The verse displays the wall: broken down. The verse displays the barrier: the ceremonial law. The reconciliation displays the dividing wall removed in Christ. The verse displays the peace: he is. The verse displays the both: made one. The verse displays the partition: broken. Christ our peace, breaking the middle wall between Jew and Gentile, displays the heart of the reconciliation: Christ is himself the peace, who has made the two long-divided peoples one and broken down the dividing wall — the ceremonial barrier — that kept them apart. The verse displays the uniting of Jew and Gentile, by abolishing the law of ordinances (v.15).
15:
"Having abolished in his flesh the enmity, even the law of commandments contained in ordinances; for to make in himself of twain one new man, so making peace;" The witnesses note the law of ordinances abolished to make one new man. On "abolished in his flesh the enmity, even the law of commandments contained in ordinances," the grammatical voices note what was removed. Barnes and Robertson note that Christ "abolished in his flesh (by his death) the enmity (the cause of hostility between Jew and Gentile), even the law of commandments contained in ordinances" — the ceremonial law, with its rites and observances, which had been the dividing barrier; this Christ annulled.

On "to make in himself of twain one new man," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note God's design: "to make in himself of twain (the two, Jew and Gentile) one new man" — not Jew absorbing Gentile or the reverse, but both fused into a wholly "new man," a new humanity in Christ.

On "so making peace," the grammatical-historical note the result: by abolishing the dividing law and creating one new man, Christ made peace between the two. The verse displays the law of ordinances abolished to make one new man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ "abolished in his flesh (by his death) the enmity, even the law of commandments contained in ordinances" — the ceremonial law, the dividing barrier, annulled (Barnes, Robertson); his design "to make in himself of twain (Jew and Gentile) one new man," both fused into a new humanity in Christ, not one absorbing the other (Barnes, Calvin); "so making peace," peace the result (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the law abolished, one new man made. The verse marks the abolition: the law of ordinances. The verse displays the enmity: removed. The verse displays the purpose: one new man. The verse displays the result: peace. The abolition displays the dividing law removed to make a new humanity. The verse displays the ordinances: abolished. The verse displays the new man: made. The verse displays the peace: made. The law of ordinances abolished to make one new man displays the means of the reconciliation: Christ, by his death, annulled the ceremonial law that bred enmity between Jew and Gentile, in order to fuse the two into one new man in himself — so making peace. The verse displays the new humanity in Christ, reconciled to God in one body (v.16).
16:
"And that he might reconcile both unto God in one body by the cross, having slain the enmity thereby:" The brief verse notes both reconciled to God in one body by the cross. The grammatical voices note the further purpose. Barnes and Robertson note the second design: "that he might reconcile both (Jew and Gentile) unto God in one body by the cross" — not only uniting the two to each other but reconciling both, now one body, to God himself, through the cross.

On "in one body," the witnesses note the unity. Barnes and Calvin note that the reconciliation is "in one body" — the two are reconciled to God together, as one new body (the church); their union with each other and their reconciliation to God are bound together.

On "having slain the enmity thereby," the Reformed Calvin notes the death of hostility: "having slain the enmity (the hostility, both between the two and toward God) thereby (by the cross)" — the cross put the enmity to death, abolishing both the division and the alienation from God. The verse displays both reconciled to God in one body by the cross.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second design — "that he might reconcile both (Jew and Gentile) unto God in one body by the cross," not only uniting the two but reconciling both, now one body, to God (Barnes, Robertson); "in one body," the two reconciled together as one new body the church (Barnes, Calvin); "having slain the enmity thereby," the cross putting hostility to death, abolishing division and alienation (Calvin). The verse displays both reconciled to God. The verse marks the reconciliation: both to God. The verse displays the unity: in one body. The verse displays the means: by the cross. The verse displays the enmity: slain. The reconciliation displays the two made one and reconciled to God. The verse displays the both: reconciled. The verse displays the body: one. The verse displays the cross: the means. Both reconciled to God in one body by the cross display the fulness of the reconciliation: Christ not only united Jew and Gentile but reconciled both, now one body, to God himself through the cross — slaying by it the enmity that divided them from each other and from God. The verse displays the reconciliation of all to God, proclaimed as peace to near and far (v.17).
17:
"And came and preached peace to you which were afar off, and to them that were nigh." The brief verse notes Christ preaching peace to far and near. The grammatical voices note the proclamation. Barnes and Robertson note that Christ "came and preached (proclaimed, announced — by his coming, his Spirit, and his apostles) peace to you which were afar off (the Gentiles) and to them that were nigh (the Jews)" — the gospel of peace announced to both.

On "afar off... nigh," the witnesses note the two groups. Barnes and Chrysostom note the echo of Isaiah 57:19: peace is proclaimed both to the "afar off" (the Gentiles, distant from the covenant) and the "nigh" (the Jews, who had the covenant) — the same peace to both, none excluded.

The grammatical-historical note the means: Christ "came and preached" — both in his own person and through his messengers; the gospel is the announcement of the peace he made. The verse displays Christ preaching peace to far and near.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ "came and preached (proclaimed) peace to you which were afar off (the Gentiles) and to them that were nigh (the Jews)," the gospel of peace announced to both (Barnes, Robertson); the echo of Isaiah 57:19, the same peace to far and near, none excluded (Barnes, Chrysostom); preached by Christ in person and through his messengers (grammatical-historical). The verse displays peace preached to all. The verse marks the proclamation: preached peace. The verse displays the far: the Gentiles. The verse displays the near: the Jews. The verse displays the means: Christ and his messengers. The preaching displays peace announced to both groups. The verse displays the peace: proclaimed. The verse displays the afar off: reached. The verse displays the nigh: included. Christ preaching peace to far and near displays the universal proclamation of the reconciliation: Christ came and announced peace — through himself and his apostles — both to the Gentiles who were far off and to the Jews who were near, the same gospel peace offered to both. The verse displays the preaching of peace, opening access to the Father (v.18).
18:
"For through him we both have access by one Spirit unto the Father." The witnesses note both having access to the Father through Christ by one Spirit. On "through him we both have access," the grammatical voices note the way to God. Barnes and Robertson note that "through him (Christ) we both (Jew and Gentile) have access (prosagoge — the right of approach, introduction, as into a king's presence)" — Christ is the one way of approach to God for both peoples.

On "by one Spirit unto the Father," the witnesses note the Trinity and the unity. Barnes and Calvin note the threefold structure: access is "through him (the Son)," "by one Spirit (the Holy Spirit)," "unto the Father" — a clear trinitarian witness; and the "one Spirit" marks the unity, both having the same access by the same Spirit.

The grammatical-historical note the privilege: the once-distant Gentiles now share with the Jews the highest privilege — free access to God as Father, through the Son and by the Spirit. The verse displays both having access to the Father through Christ by one Spirit.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "through him (Christ) we both (Jew and Gentile) have access (the right of approach, introduction)," Christ the one way to God for both (Barnes, Robertson); the threefold structure "through the Son, by one Spirit, unto the Father" a trinitarian witness, the "one Spirit" marking their unity (Barnes, Calvin); the Gentiles now sharing the highest privilege, free access to God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays access to the Father. The verse marks the access: through Christ. The verse displays the means: by one Spirit. The verse displays the goal: unto the Father. The verse displays the Trinity: Son, Spirit, Father. The access displays both approaching God through the Trinity. The verse displays the access: granted. The verse displays the Spirit: one. The verse displays the Father: approached. Both having access to the Father through Christ by one Spirit displays the crowning privilege of the reconciliation: Jew and Gentile alike now have free access to God as Father — through the Son, by one Spirit — a trinitarian way of approach in which the once-divided peoples are united. The verse displays the shared access to God, making the Gentiles fellow-citizens (vv.19-22).
19:
"Now therefore ye are no more strangers and foreigners, but fellowcitizens with the saints, and of the household of God;" The witnesses note the Gentiles now fellow-citizens and members of God's household. On "no more strangers and foreigners," the grammatical voices note the reversal. Barnes and Robertson note the conclusion ("now therefore"): the Gentiles are "no more strangers (xenoi — aliens) and foreigners (paroikoi — resident aliens, sojourners without civic rights)" — their former alienation (v.12) is ended.

On "fellowcitizens with the saints," the witnesses note the citizenship. Barnes and Calvin note that they are now "fellowcitizens with the saints" — full citizens of God's commonwealth, sharing equal rights with all the people of God; the once-excluded are enfranchised.

On "of the household of God," the puritan Henry notes the family: more than citizens, they are "of the household (the family) of God" — members of God's own family, with the intimacy and privileges of children in his house. The verse displays the Gentiles now fellow-citizens and members of God's household.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Gentiles are "no more strangers (aliens) and foreigners (resident sojourners without rights)," their former alienation ended (Barnes, Robertson); now "fellowcitizens with the saints," full citizens of God's commonwealth with equal rights (Barnes, Calvin); and "of the household (family) of God," members of his own family with a child's privileges (Henry). The verse displays fellow-citizens and family. The verse marks the reversal: no more strangers. The verse displays the citizenship: fellow-citizens. The verse displays the family: household of God. The verse displays the privilege: enfranchised and adopted. The conclusion displays the Gentiles made citizens and family. The verse displays the strangers: no more. The verse displays the citizens: with the saints. The verse displays the household: of God. The Gentiles now fellow-citizens and members of God's household display the height of their new standing: no longer aliens and sojourners, they are full citizens with all the saints and members of God's own family — the once-excluded now enfranchised and adopted. The verse displays the new citizenship and family, built on the apostolic foundation (v.20).
20:
"And are built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone;" The witnesses note the church built on the apostolic foundation, Christ the corner stone. On "built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets," the grammatical voices note the foundation. Barnes and Robertson note the figure shifts to a building: believers are "built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets" — either the foundation that the apostles and prophets laid (their doctrine, the gospel they preached), or the foundation which they themselves are; the "prophets" being most likely the New Testament prophets (so Ellicott, Robertson), or the Old Testament prophets (others).

On "Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone," the witnesses note the corner. Barnes and Calvin note that "Jesus Christ himself" is "the chief corner stone (akrogoniaios)" — the stone that binds the two walls (Jew and Gentile) together and supports the whole; Christ is the foundation and bond of the building.

The grammatical-historical note the unity: the corner stone joining the two walls fitly pictures Christ uniting Jew and Gentile into one temple. The verse displays the church built on the apostolic foundation, Christ the corner stone.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: believers are "built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets" — the doctrine they laid (the gospel), the "prophets" likely the NT prophets (Ellicott, Robertson) or OT (others) (Barnes, Robertson); "Jesus Christ himself" the "chief corner stone" that binds the two walls (Jew and Gentile) and supports the whole (Barnes, Calvin); the corner stone fitly picturing Christ uniting the two into one temple (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the apostolic foundation. The verse marks the foundation: apostles and prophets. The verse displays the corner: Christ. The verse displays the binding: of two walls. The verse displays the unity: one temple. The building displays the church founded on the apostles, cornered on Christ. The verse displays the foundation: apostolic. The verse displays the corner stone: Christ. The verse displays the building: united. The church built on the apostolic foundation, Christ the corner stone, displays the structure of the new people of God: believers are built on the foundation laid by the apostles and prophets, with Jesus Christ himself as the chief corner stone — the bond that joins Jew and Gentile and supports the whole building. The verse displays the foundation and corner of the temple, which grows into a holy sanctuary (v.21).
21:
"In whom all the building fitly framed together groweth unto an holy temple in the Lord:" The brief verse notes the building growing into a holy temple in the Lord. The grammatical voices note the structure. Barnes and Robertson note that "in whom (in Christ) all the building, fitly framed together (synarmologoumene — joined and fitted, each stone in its place) groweth (auxei — grows, as a living organism)" — the church is a building that, joined in Christ, grows like a living thing.

On "groweth unto an holy temple in the Lord," the witnesses note the goal. Barnes and Calvin note that the building "groweth unto an holy temple (naos — a sanctuary, the dwelling-place of God) in the Lord" — the church is becoming a holy temple, consecrated to God, in union with the Lord; the goal of the growth is to be God's sanctuary.

The grammatical-historical note the union: the fitting-together and growth happen "in him" and "in the Lord" — Christ is the principle of both the cohesion and the increase of the temple. The verse displays the building growing into a holy temple in the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "in Christ all the building, fitly framed together (joined and fitted, each stone in place) groweth (as a living organism)," the church a building that grows like a living thing in Christ (Barnes, Robertson); "unto an holy temple (a sanctuary, God's dwelling) in the Lord," becoming a holy temple consecrated to God (Barnes, Calvin); the fitting and growth happening in Christ, the principle of both (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the growing temple. The verse marks the framing: fitly joined. The verse displays the growth: like a living thing. The verse displays the goal: a holy temple. The verse displays the union: in the Lord. The building displays the church growing into God's sanctuary in Christ. The verse displays the building: framed. The verse displays the growth: organic. The verse displays the temple: holy. The building growing into a holy temple in the Lord displays the living nature of the church: in Christ all the stones are fitly joined together and grow, like a living organism, into a holy temple — a sanctuary consecrated to God in union with the Lord. The verse displays the growing temple of God, in which the Gentiles too are built (v.22).
22:
"In whom ye also are builded together for an habitation of God through the Spirit." The witnesses note the Gentiles built together as God's dwelling by the Spirit. On "in whom ye also are builded together," the grammatical voices note the inclusion. Barnes and Robertson note the application to the Gentile believers: "in whom (in Christ) ye also (the Gentiles) are builded together (synoikodomeisthe — built up together with the rest)" — the Gentiles are incorporated into the same building, fellow-stones in God's temple.

On "for an habitation of God through the Spirit," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the goal: the building is "for an habitation (katoiketerion — a dwelling-place, a permanent residence) of God through (by, in) the Spirit" — the church is the temple in which God dwells, not by outward symbols but by his Spirit; God inhabits his people through the Holy Spirit.

The grammatical-historical note the climax: the whole passage culminates here — the once-far-off Gentiles, with the Jews, are now the very dwelling-place of God by his Spirit; the highest conceivable dignity. The verse displays the Gentiles built together as God's dwelling by the Spirit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Gentile believers — "in Christ ye also are builded together (built up with the rest)" — incorporated into the same building, fellow-stones in God's temple (Barnes, Robertson); "for an habitation (a dwelling-place) of God through the Spirit," the church the temple in which God dwells by his Spirit, not by symbols (Barnes, Calvin); the once-far-off Gentiles now the very dwelling of God, the highest dignity (grammatical-historical). The verse displays God's dwelling by the Spirit. The verse marks the inclusion: ye also built together. The verse displays the purpose: a habitation of God. The verse displays the means: through the Spirit. The verse displays the climax: God's dwelling-place. The building displays the Gentiles made God's temple by the Spirit. The verse displays the building: together. The verse displays the habitation: of God. The verse displays the Spirit: the means. The Gentiles built together as God's dwelling by the Spirit display the crowning dignity of the reconciliation: incorporated with the Jews into the one building in Christ, the once-far-off Gentiles are now the very habitation of God — the temple in which he dwells by his Holy Spirit. The verse displays the church as God's Spirit-indwelt temple, closing the great chapter on grace and reconciliation.
